title: Camp Meeting by Alexander Rider
caption: This black and white drawing depicts a large revival meeting, with tents in the background and a large wooden pulpit in the foreground. Camp meetings were a popular form of Protestant worship throughout the 19th century. Lasting several days, these open-air events often involved ecstatic communal prayer. Hundreds and even thousands came from miles around for preaching and worship, and to enjoy the festival-like atmosphere. A large crowd is depicted as gathered in the foreground. Some people are sitting on benches, while others kneel on the ground or stand listening to a preacher.
collection_title: Home and Community Life: Domestic Life

title: [Camp Meeting] by Hugh Bridport (graphic artist) and Kennedy & Lucas after a painting by Alexander Rider
caption: This black and white print depicts a large revival or camp meeting, with tents in the background and a large wooden pulpit in the foreground. Camp meetings were a popular form of Protestant worship throughout the 19th century. Lasting several days, these open-air events often involved ecstatic communal prayer. Hundreds and even thousands came from miles around for preaching and worship, and to enjoy the festival-like atmosphere. A large crowd is gathered in the foreground. Some people are sitting on benches, while others are kneeling on the ground or standing listening to a preacher. The title is cut off. In the upper right corner, a newspaper clipping describing a revival meeting is affixed to the print.
collection_title: Home and Community Life: Domestic Life

title: Camp-Meeting by Hugh Bridport and and Kennedy & Lucas after a painting by Alexander Rider
caption: This colored print depicts a large revival meeting, with tents in the background and a large wooden pulpit in the foreground. Camp meetings were a popular form of Protestant worship throughout the 19th century. Lasting several days, these open-air events often involved ecstatic communal prayer. Hundreds and even thousands came from miles around for preaching and worship, and to enjoy the festival-like atmosphere. A large crowd is depicted in the foreground. Some people are sitting on benches, while others kneel on the ground or stand listening to a preacher.
collection_title: Home and Community Life: Domestic Life

title: Baptising Scene by Endicott & Swett
caption: This exceptional colored print depicts a woman being baptized by a minister in the Hudson River, north of Manhattan. A large group of well-dressed, white Protestant parishioners are watching from the riverbank and a nearby dock. Three black children stand clustered together in the center foreground.
collection_title: Home and Community Life: Domestic Life

title: Certificate of baptism signed by Rev. Volley V. K. Stokes
caption: Baptismal certificate used by Rev. Volley Stokes. The form is partially completed. The top of the form reads [Certificate of Baptism/ This Certifies] surrounded by a curvilinear design. The middle section of the paper is not filled out. The standard printed text reads [That ______ / of ______ / born on the ____ day of ______ 19__]. Below is [RECEIVED / Christian Baptism] followed by a completed section with handwritten information in blue and black ink. The text reads [on the 12th day of April in / the year of our Lord 1940 / at Trinity Baptist Church, Balt. Md / Rev. Volley V.K. Stokes, B.D. Pastor]. The publisher is printed in the bottom left. The back is blank.
collection_title: National Museum of African American History and Culture Collection

title: Butterfly Basket
caption: Constructed in the shape of a butterfly, this basket includes double plied twisted seagrass cord, wood splints, and round reeds woven in a plain weave. This variety of materials gives the piece texture and provides a slight variation in color, as the seagrass cord is darker than the splints and round reeds. The handle is covered with a plain weave and reed weft.

title: Photographic postcard of J. J. Cotten Barbershop
caption: A black-and-white photograph postcard of a thriving barbershop in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, circa 1910. Captured in the midst of various grooming services, this rare portrait shows a small but full shop; men being shaved, one man having his shoes shined, two men standing at a glad counter, and several other men seated or standing – and all looking towards the camera. Barbershops played a vital role in the cultural and economic development of African American communities. It was a space where African American men interacted with each other, regardless of age, class, education, occupation, etc., serving as the backbone of many black communities and their role of strengthening black male identity in America was vital.
collection_title: National Museum of African American History and Culture Collection

title: Charles Meigs
caption: The Frederick Hill Meserve Collection comprises more than five thousand Civil War-era portrait negatives from the Mathew Brady photography studio in New York City. The collection, which the National Portrait Gallery acquired in 1981, includes portraits of generals, politicians, diplomats, painters, and performers. It also contains depictions of “Human Curiosities” at P. T. Barnum’s American Museum in New York City, that, although highly exploitative, help to document the historical representations of disability in the United States.

title: Fortune Teller at the Louisiana State Fair
caption: A fortune teller becomes part of the state fair machinery: spectacle, labor, belief, and small-town commerce all compressed into one booth.

title: Undertaker Before the Service
caption: The undertaker stands inside the ordinary logistics of mourning, making death care feel less symbolic than procedural.

title: River Baptism Waiting Line
caption: The image treats baptism as a communal physical ordeal: bodies, white clothing, river water, and waiting become the evidence of belief.

title: Woman Night Guard with Dog
caption: A woman, a dog, and the title ‘Night Watch’ turn security work into a strange civic ritual of vigilance.

title: Truck Stop Attendant on U.S. 1
caption: The attendant belongs to a roadside world of fuel, trucks, waiting, and small acts of service that usually disappear from history.

title: Gas Yard Attendant in Mississippi
caption: A petroleum yard portrait where the workplace matters as much as the person, preserving a local industrial world in color.

title: Keeper of the Bustami Shrine
caption: The shrine keeper suggests a whole religious infrastructure of caretaking, access, memory, and local authority.

title: Temple Watchman at Kurodani Monastery
caption: A temple watchman makes religious space feel maintained and guarded rather than merely picturesque.

title: Puppeteer with Hush Puppy and Lamb Chop
caption: The puppeteer appears inside the working grammar of children’s television, where fabricated companions become professional partners.

title: Medicine Show in Huntingdon
caption: A traveling medicine show turns commerce into theater, revealing the blurred line between entertainment, cure, and persuasion.

title: Carpenter with Tools
caption: The portrait uses tools as biography, making the carpenter’s trade inseparable from his public identity.

title: Blacksmith Holding Horseshoe
caption: The blacksmith’s hammer, pliers, and horseshoe create a compact visual contract between person, trade, and material labor.

title: Seamstress at the Machine
caption: The sewing machine anchors the portrait in skilled domestic labor, giving a quiet occupation the gravity of invention.

title: Cooper with Barrel and Tools
caption: The barrel maker’s portrait preserves a material trade whose evidence is almost entirely in the object beside him.

title: Woman with Broom
caption: A broom becomes the entire occupational world, turning maintenance labor into a formal act of witness.

title: Peddler Carrying His Wares
caption: The peddler’s body is rigged to his trade, making mobility, burden, and salesmanship visible at once.

title: Watchmaker at the Table
caption: Tiny objects and a worktable turn the portrait toward patience, repair, and the almost invisible labor of keeping time.

title: Henry the College Janitor
caption: A college janitor appears as part of the institution’s hidden operating system, the worker who makes the formal world possible.

title: Former Senator as Capitol Janitor
caption: The caption’s role reversal—senator becoming janitor—makes maintenance work collide with political status.

title: Census Worker at the Door
caption: The census appears as a doorstep ritual: government counting becomes an encounter between clipboard, household, and threshold.

title: Storeroom Keeper After the Crash
caption: A ruined financier working in a municipal lodging house turns economic collapse into an occupational portrait.

title: Zoo Keeper with Pygmy Hippos
caption: The keeper stands inside a staged encounter between animal care, science publicity, and press spectacle.

title: Security Guard at Black Lives Matter Plaza
caption: A lone guard before a boarded building turns protection work into a record of civic tension and ordinary endurance.

title: S.W. Pass Lighthouse and Keeper’s House
caption: The keeper’s house and boardwalk make maritime safety visible as a lived workplace, not just a beacon.

title: Mexican Grandmother Picking Tomatoes
caption: The field portrait places age, family migration, and agricultural labor in the same frame without turning any of them into abstraction.

title: Sojourner Truth at the Table
caption: The table, book, and knitting resist pure celebrity portraiture, grounding a public figure in objects of labor, thought, and self-presentation.

title: Spirit Medium Exposé Title Page
caption: A spiritualist exposure text turns fraud, performance, and belief into one strange printed artifact.

title: Houdini With the Apparition of Lincoln
caption: A staged ghost photograph becomes more compelling as evidence of belief, debunking, and showmanship at once.

title: Puppets After Hours
caption: The image imagines puppets continuing their drama after the people leave, making performance feel secretly alive.

title: Puppet Theater With Fran Allison and Kukla and Ollie
caption: A performer and puppets share the frame as a working relationship rather than a simple publicity portrait.

title: Fortune Teller Poster
caption: The poster turns divination into mass spectacle, advertising mystery as something you could buy and hang.

title: Fortune Teller, Chinatown, San Francisco
caption: The setting keeps the figure from becoming a studio type, grounding divination in public street life.

title: Fortune Teller at Louisiana State Fair
caption: A fairground diviner becomes a complete character because the booth, signage, and setting do half the storytelling.

title: Fortune Teller's Cubicle, Louisiana State Fair
caption: The empty cubicle is stronger than a generic portrait because it preserves the working room of the performance.

title: Medicine Show, Huntingdon, Tennessee
caption: A traveling medicine show turns commerce, entertainment, and public trust into one portable roadside world.

title: Security Guard at Nuclear Power Plant
caption: The guard's presence is amplified by the fence and power plant, making security feel like an occupation at the edge of invisible danger.

title: Embalming Surgeon at Work
caption: The image makes death care brutally procedural, showing preservation as labor rather than symbolism.
